scope_delivery.explanation: Yes. Plan declared scope was a two-line edit on engine/session/auto_target.json (P5-06 status flip + blocked_reason clear) plus an out-of-repo UI prompt update the user owns. Both Step 1 (the auto_target.json edit) and Step 3 (T1-A verification) are now complete — Step 1 landed in commit d2edab6; Step 3 was verified end-to-end during plan authoring when the routine fire executed against S-0066, exercised apply_migration.py cleanly, and closed T1-A. Step 2 (UI prompt update) remains user-owned out-of-repo. Recovery from the stale-claim collision was named explicitly in the plan-relative outcome (the plan said 'commit S-0066'; the routine got S-0066; this session pivoted to S-0067 cleanly with explicit user authorization for the destructive action) — not a scope expansion, the same logical work landed.

unblock P5-06 — Issue #18 resolved at S-0064; T1-A verified at S-0066

side_discoveries: Stale-claim collision shape distinct from ADR 0052's eager-claim race. ADR 0052's routine_eager_claim_recovery.py mechanically verifies a narrow recovery (HEAD = exactly one commit ahead of origin/main, both commits being eager-claims for the same slot). When a routine fires AND closes during another session's planning window, the rival session may then attempt to eager-claim the same slot post-rival-close — origin/main HEAD is a *close* commit, not an eager-claim commit, so the recovery tool's shape verifier (correctly) refuses. Manual git reset --hard origin/main with explicit user authorization is the right path here. Worth a posture-doc note that ADR 0052's coverage is the FIRE-during-FIRE collision, not the FIRE-during-PLANNING collision; the latter is structurally rarer (interactive sessions read state at boot and would normally see 'closed' before claiming) but happens when planning is long enough to span a routine fire. Not urgent to mechanize — the manual reset takes one user-authorized command.

Routine UI prompt drift named 'session-build-lifecycle skill in routine mode' — this skill does not exist for routine fires. The actual routine skill is 'routine-mode-lifecycle' (per .claude/skills/routine-mode-lifecycle/SKILL.md, frontmatter: disable-model-invocation: true). Routine fires reading this misleading entry point have to recover. Plan recommended replacing UI prompt with just /start-routine (the slash command body is the canonical comprehensive boot procedure). User owns the UI edit; out-of-repo. Worth pairing with a CLAUDE.md or operations-doc bullet noting: 'When the AI inside a routine fire reads a UI prompt that diverges from the slash command body, treat the slash command as canonical.'
